epigraph.text:
Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, 
as working for the Lord.
epigraph.quote: Mathematics is not about numbers, equations, computations, or algorithms: it is about understanding.

content.text: {Mathematics is not about numbers, equations, computations, or algorithms: it is about understanding.}{William Paul Thurston}

**Factoring** is the process of writing a polynomial as a product 
of simpler expressions. It reverses multiplication.

items: The GCF is the largest factor common to all terms
Factor out the GCF first, always
Sometimes the common factor is a binomial
For four-term polynomials with no overall GCF
Group into pairs, factor each pair
Look for a common binomial factor
For $x^2 + bx + c$, find $m$ and $n$ where $m + n = b$ and $mn = c$
AC Method: multiply $ac$, split middle term, factor by grouping
Trial and Error: systematically test factor combinations
Sum/difference of cubes: $a^3 \pm b^3 = (a \pm b)(a^2 \mp ab + b^2)$
Count terms and apply appropriate technique

content.text:
Factoring is one of the most important skills in algebra. It transforms 
complex expressions into simpler components, revealing structure that 
was hidden. In Chapter 9, you will see how factoring enables us to 
solve quadratic equations—finding the values of $x$ that make 
$x^2 + bx + c = 0$.

Consider: mathematics often progresses by taking complicated objects 
and breaking them into simpler pieces. Prime factorization breaks 
numbers into primes. Factoring polynomials breaks expressions into 
irreducible components. This reductionist approach—understanding 
the whole by understanding its parts—reflects a deep pattern in 
how God has structured knowable reality.

Yet there is also synthesis: from simple pieces, complex wholes emerge. 
Both analysis and synthesis, taking apart and building up, are essential 
to mathematical understanding. As you master factoring, you gain the 
power to move freely in both directions.

Factoring reveals hidden structure in polynomials. 
Consider how understanding the "building blocks" of something—whether 
numbers, expressions, or ideas—can lead to deeper insight. How might 
this principle apply beyond mathematics?

marginNotes.text:
Great are the works of the Lord, studied 
by all who delight in them.

Prepare your work outside; get 
everything ready for yourself in the field, and after that build 
your house.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a place to stand, and I will move 
the earth.
